title: Egyptian Koshari

ingredients.name: masoor dal (red or brown lentils), rinsed
visual_checks: Traditional koshari uses brown lentils that hold shape; masoor gives a softer result
ingredients.notes: Traditional koshari uses brown lentils that hold shape; masoor gives a softer result

subs.name: Yellow moong dal for a softer texture; or substitute half the lentils with additional chickpeas

ingredients.name: small elbow macaroni or broken vermicelli

subs.name: Broken wheat semiya (vermicelli) is the most authentic and widely available Indian substitute; cook in boiling water for 3–4 minutes
Cooked rajma (kidney beans) or boiled kala chana for a different but equally hearty protein layer

ingredients.name: onion, halved and very thinly sliced

subs.name: Fresh lime juice thinned with a little water for a citrus version of the sharp sauce

steps.text: Start with the crispy onions as they take the longest. Heat 2 tbsp oil in a large kadai or frying pan over medium-low heat. Add the thinly sliced onions with a pinch of salt and cook slowly, stirring every few minutes, for 20 minutes until deep golden-brown and crispy — patience here is key. Drain on paper towels and set aside; they will crisp further as they cool.

While the onions cook, bring 500 ml salted water to a boil in a saucepan. Add the masoor dal and cook for 12–15 minutes until tender but the lentils still hold their shape (don't let them turn to mush). Drain and set aside.
Cook the macaroni in a separate pot of salted boiling water until al dente (check packet time, usually 8–10 minutes). Drain and set aside.

Make the tomato sauce: heat 2 tbsp oil in a saucepan. Add half the minced garlic and stir for 30 seconds. Add tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes. Add fresh tomato, 1 tsp cumin, coriander powder and chilli powder; simmer uncovered for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens and the raw tomato smell is gone. Season with salt.

Make the garlic-vinegar sauce: combine the vinegar, remaining garlic (minced), a pinch of cumin and a pinch of salt in a small bowl. Stir and set aside — this sharp sauce is drizzled over the bowl at serving.

Cook the rice: rinse the rice, combine with 1.75 cups water, 0.5 tsp cumin, and 0.5 tsp salt in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, then lower to the minimum flame, cover and cook for 18 minutes until all water is absorbed and the rice is fluffy. Fluff with a fork.

Assemble: layer a generous portion of cumin rice in each bowl, add a spoonful of lentils and some macaroni on top, then scatter chickpeas over. Ladle the spiced tomato sauce generously over everything. Pile the crispy onions on top and drizzle the garlic-vinegar sauce to taste. Serve immediately so the onions stay crunchy.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: pasta → Broken wheat semiya (vermicelli) is the most authentic and widely available Indian substitute; cook in boiling water for 3–4 minutes; masoor-dal → Yellow moong dal for a softer texture; or substitute half the lentils with additional chickpeas; chickpea → Cooked rajma (kidney beans) or boiled kala chana for a different but equally hearty protein layer; vinegar → Fresh lime juice thinned with a little water for a citrus version of the sharp sauce
indian_kitchen_adaptation: All components are entirely achievable in an Indian kitchen. Use sona masoori or short-grain rice. Replace macaroni with semiya (wheat vermicelli) — widely available and arguably more authentic. The birista technique (slow-fried onions for biryani) is identical to the crispy onion topping. Cook lentils in a pressure cooker for 2 whistles if short on time.
